stocks.note: Numbers as of market close April 8th.
stocks.news:
Markets ripped higher as the ceasefire entered day two. Meta surged 6.5% after unveiling Muse Spark — its first heavyweight AI model, built to power features across Facebook, Instagram, WhatsApp, and the Ray-Ban smart glasses. The plan? Prove the model works in-house, then sell access to outside companies.

Delta climbed 3.75% after posting better-than-expected revenue and earnings, even with oil hovering near $110 a barrel during the quarter. Premium and business travelers kept booking through war, oil spikes, and winter storms like nothing happened.

And the billionaire cage match continues — Elon Musk is now pushing the courts to remove Sam Altman as OpenAI’s CEO, escalating the lawsuit he filed last year accusing Altman of deception around early donations to the company.

economy.commentary:
Gold posted its best single day since November, blasting past $4,800 — and it did it while stocks were rallying. That’s unusual. The read? Investors aren’t fully convinced the ceasefire will hold, so they’re grabbing gold as a hedge.

The Fed’s March meeting notes confirmed at least one rate cut is still on the table this year. The ceasefire pushed December cut odds to 43% on CME, with a Fed study suggesting inflation could drop quickly if Middle East oil starts flowing again.

But don’t expect a quick recovery at the pump. Even with the Strait of Hormuz reopening, experts say restarting wells could take 6 to 9 months, and roughly 1,000 ships remain stuck waiting to pass through. The 11 million barrels per day that went offline won’t come back overnight.

President Trump added another wrinkle — any country supplying weapons to Iran will now face 50% tariffs on all goods sold to the U.S. No names were named, but Russia and China are squarely in the crosshairs.

crypto.commentary:
Morgan Stanley just entered the ring with a spot Bitcoin ETF — the first from a major U.S. investment bank. The kicker? Fees are nearly half what BlackRock charges on its $55 billion Bitcoin fund.

Meanwhile, someone’s been buying the war dip. Around 850,000 Bitcoin — roughly 4% of total supply — have changed hands since January. That’s tightened available supply significantly, and if demand picks up, the math favors anyone who loaded up.

real_estate.homebuilder: Rates dip to 6.40% as the ceasefire cools inflation fears — spring buyers have a narrow window before the next move.
real_estate.commentary:
Total inventory across our 16-city tracker climbed to 2,555 — up 60 from yesterday. Coming soon listings at 247 and 193 new listings as spring sellers ramp up.

📍 Fremont: Up to 305 (from 297) with 26 new listings and 56 coming soon. Spring is clearly in motion.

📍 Union City: Slipped to 79 — still one of the tightest markets on the board.

📍 Oakland: 733 total with 41 new listings. The Bay’s biggest inventory pool keeps growing.

📍 Milpitas: Holding at 115 with 33 coming soon but only 5 new. Heavy pipeline, light on fresh supply.

📍 Newark: Climbed to 102 with 7 new listings — just cracked triple digits.

📍 Hayward: 216 total with 13 new. Consistent supply for buyers looking east.

Mortgage applications fell for the third straight week, even with rates drifting down from the 6.47% high in late March. Some analysts think if the Iran ceasefire sticks, rates could test 6% by June.

Nationally, 13 metro areas now have a median price above $1 million — five of them in California. But even at the top, prices are softening: the top 10% of U.S. home prices fell 2.9% year-over-year.

⚠️ Rabies Warning in Glenmoor: Health officials and the Fremont Police Department have confirmed that a dead bat found in the Glenmoor neighborhood recently tested positive for the rabies virus. Residents are advised to be cautious, avoid handling wild animals, and ensure their pets’ vaccinations are up to date.

👮 New Police Chief: Floyd Mitchell has officially taken the helm as Fremont’s new Police Chief, bringing his leadership experience from the Oakland Police Department.

🏛️ Charter City Transition: Fremont is actively exploring a transition from a general law city to a Charter City. The newly appointed Charter Advisory Committee held its latest public meeting on April 6 to discuss potential changes to local governance.
